content.text: RAG Reference Architecture for Enterprise Claude

Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) is the dominant pattern for grounding Claude's responses in an organisation's internal knowledge. Rather than relying on Claude's training data alone, a RAG integration retrieves relevant documents from a curated knowledge base at query time and includes them in the context window. This allows Claude to reason accurately over documents it was not trained on, produce answers that cite specific sources, and stay within a defined scope of knowledge.

The reference architecture described here covers all the components required to build a production-ready enterprise RAG integration with Claude. Each component has specific design considerations that differ from standard software systems; the sections below address those considerations in the order they arise when building the system.

attrs.alt: Enterprise RAG reference architecture: user → API gateway → query processor → retriever → Claude API; knowledge base → ingestion pipeline → embedding service → vector store; observability layer

content.text: Component Descriptions
API Gateway
The API gateway is the entry point for all requests. For enterprise Claude integrations, the gateway must handle:
Authentication and authorisation:

Every request must be authenticated and the user's entitlement to query the knowledge base confirmed. For integrations where the knowledge base contains documents with different access levels, the gateway passes the user's identity and group memberships to the query processor, which uses them to filter retrieved documents.
Rate limiting:

Per-user and per-team rate limits prevent a single user or team from consuming the entire API tier capacity. The rate limits are enforced at the gateway, before any embedding or Claude API call is made.
Request logging:

Every request is logged at the gateway with a request ID, user identity (hashed for privacy if required), timestamp, and query length. The request ID is threaded through all downstream components to enable full-trace debugging.
Query Processor

The query processor is responsible for transforming the raw user query into a form that produces good retrieval results. The simplest query processor passes the query directly to the retriever — but this approach degrades at scale as queries become more varied and complex.
Query classification:

Classify the query into a type (factual lookup, analytical question, procedural request) to route it to the appropriate retrieval strategy. A factual lookup benefits from high-precision retrieval; an analytical question may require retrieving multiple document sections and synthesising across them.
Query rewriting:

For conversational interfaces, the user's latest message may be underspecified ('what about the termination clause?') without the conversation context. The query processor expands the query using the conversation history to produce a self-contained retrieval query.
Scope enforcement:

If the integration has a defined scope (it answers only contract-related questions, not general business questions), the query processor enforces that scope before making any retrieval call. Queries outside scope are returned with a scope-redirect response without making an API call.
Retriever
The retriever executes the vector similarity search against the knowledge base and selects the document chunks to include in the Claude context window.
Top-K retrieval:

Retrieve the K most similar document chunks to the query embedding. The value of K is a parameter with quality and cost implications: a higher K includes more context but increases token cost and may reduce answer focus. A typical starting value is K=5 for focused queries; K=10 for analytical queries.
Re-ranking:

Vector similarity is an imperfect relevance signal. A cross-encoder re-ranking model scores each retrieved chunk against the original query and re-orders the results. Re-ranking improves precision at the cost of additional latency (~50–100ms for K=10). It is recommended for integrations where precision matters more than latency.
Access filtering:

For knowledge bases with access-controlled documents, the retriever filters results to include only chunks from documents the user is authorised to access. This filter is applied after retrieval (post-filter) or as a metadata filter during retrieval (pre-filter), depending on the vector store's filtering capabilities.
Embedding Service

The embedding service converts text into vector representations for both the knowledge base ingestion pipeline (document chunks) and the query path (query embedding). The same embedding model must be used for both paths — embedding a query with a different model than the documents produces retrieval results that are meaningless.
For enterprise integrations, the embedding service should be:
Stateless and horizontally scalable — embedding is CPU/GPU-bound and can become a bottleneck during large knowledge base ingestion
Consistent across environments — the same model version in development, staging, and production; a model upgrade requires re-embedding the entire knowledge base
Instrumented for latency — embedding latency contributes to end-to-end response time on the query path; alert if P95 embedding latency exceeds 200ms
Vector Store

The vector store persists document embeddings and supports approximate nearest-neighbour search. The choice of vector store has implications for operational complexity, cost, and query latency at scale.
Option
Best For
Operational Complexity
Scale Characteristics
pgvector (PostgreSQL extension)
< 1M vectors; existing PostgreSQL infrastructure; teams with SQL expertise
Low — uses existing DB operations
Good to ~500k vectors; query latency degrades above 1M
Pinecone (managed)
Teams without vector DB expertise; fast time to production
Very low — fully managed
Scales to billions; consistent latency; higher cost per query
Weaviate (self-hosted)
Large vector counts; custom metadata filtering; hybrid search (vector + keyword)
Medium — requires K8s or Docker expertise
Scales well; hybrid search is a differentiator
Qdrant (self-hosted)
High-performance requirements; memory efficiency is critical
Medium
Excellent query latency; efficient memory usage
Ingestion Pipeline

The ingestion pipeline processes source documents into indexed chunks in the vector store. It runs asynchronously, independent of the query path, and is triggered by new document additions, document updates, and periodic re-indexing.

# ingestion/pipeline.py
import asyncio
from typing import AsyncIterable
from anthropic import AsyncAnthropic
import openai  # for embeddings

client = AsyncAnthropic()
embed_client = openai.AsyncOpenAI()

async def chunk_document(text: str, chunk_size: int = 512, overlap: int = 64) -> list[str]:
    # Split document text into overlapping chunks.
    words = text.split()
    chunks = []
    for i in range(0, len(words), chunk_size - overlap):
        chunk = " ".join(words[i:i + chunk_size])
        if chunk:
            chunks.append(chunk)
    return chunks

async def embed_chunks(chunks: list[str]) -> list[list[float]]:
    # Embed a batch of chunks. Batch size limited to 100.
    BATCH = 100
    embeddings = []
    for i in range(0, len(chunks), BATCH):
        batch = chunks[i:i + BATCH]
        resp = await embed_client.embeddings.create(
            input=batch, model="text-embedding-3-small"
        )
        embeddings.extend([e.embedding for e in resp.data])
    return embeddings

async def ingest_document(
    doc_id: str,
    text: str,
    metadata: dict,
    vector_store,  # your vector store client
) -> int:
    chunks = await chunk_document(text)
    embeddings = await embed_chunks(chunks)

    records = [
        {
            "id": f"{doc_id}_{i}",
            "embedding": emb,
            "text": chunk,
            "metadata": {**metadata, "chunk_index": i, "doc_id": doc_id},
        }
        for i, (chunk, emb) in enumerate(zip(chunks, embeddings))
    ]
    await vector_store.upsert(records)
    return len(records)
The Query Path: End-to-End Flow

The query path is the synchronous path a user request takes from submission to response. Every component on this path contributes to the end-to-end latency, and each adds a failure mode. The following describes the end-to-end flow with the key implementation considerations at each step.

# query/handler.py
import asyncio
from anthropic import AsyncAnthropic

client = AsyncAnthropic()

async def handle_query(
    user_query: str,
    user_id: str,
    conversation_history: list[dict],
    vector_store,
    system_prompt: str,
) -> dict:
    # Step 1: Classify and (optionally) rewrite the query
    retrieval_query = await rewrite_for_retrieval(user_query, conversation_history)

    # Step 2: Embed the retrieval query
    query_embedding = await embed_query(retrieval_query)

    # Step 3: Retrieve relevant chunks (with access filter)
    chunks = await vector_store.search(
        embedding=query_embedding,
        top_k=8,
        filter={"allowed_users": {"$contains": user_id}},
    )

    # Step 4: Re-rank
    chunks = await rerank(retrieval_query, chunks)[:5]  # keep top 5 after reranking

    # Step 5: Build context
    context_text = "\n\n---\n\n".join(
        f"[Source: {c['metadata']['doc_title']}]\n{c['text']}"
        for c in chunks
    )

    # Step 6: Call Claude
    messages = conversation_history + [
        {"role": "user", "content": f"Context:\n{context_text}\n\nQuestion: {user_query}"}
    ]
    response = await client.messages.create(
        model="claude-sonnet-4-6",
        max_tokens=1024,
        system=system_prompt,
        messages=messages,
    )

    return {
        "answer": response.content[0].text,
        "sources": [c["metadata"]["doc_title"] for c in chunks],
        "usage": response.usage.model_dump(),
    }
Knowledge Base Design Decisions

The knowledge base design — how documents are structured, chunked, and indexed — has a larger impact on RAG quality than the choice of model or vector store. Poor chunking produces poor retrieval even with a strong model.
Chunking Strategy
Fixed-size chunking (baseline):
Split documents into fixed-length chunks (e.g., 512 tokens) with overlap. Simple to implement; performs adequately for uniformly structured documents.
Semantic chunking:

Split documents at semantic boundaries (paragraph breaks, heading changes, sentence boundaries). Requires a text segmentation model but produces chunks with higher internal coherence, which improves retrieval precision.
Hierarchical chunking:

Index documents at multiple granularities: the full document (for coarse matching), sections (for medium-grain), and paragraphs (for fine-grain). At query time, retrieve at the fine-grain level but include parent section context in the Claude input.
Metadata Schema
Every chunk in the vector store should carry structured metadata that enables filtering and improves the quality of the Claude context:

# Metadata schema for each chunk record
metadata = {
    "doc_id": "contract-nda-2024-001",        # unique document identifier
    "doc_title": "Mutual NDA — Acme Corp",     # human-readable title for citations
    "doc_type": "contract",                    # enables type-based filtering
    "jurisdiction": "england-wales",           # enables jurisdiction-based filtering
    "effective_date": "2024-01-15",            # enables date-range filtering
    "access_level": "internal",               # drives access control filtering
    "owner_team": "legal",                    # for staleness alerting by content area
    "last_updated": "2024-08-20",             # for staleness detection
    "chunk_index": 3,                         # position within source document
    "total_chunks": 12,                       # total chunks in source document
}
Operational Considerations
Knowledge Base Staleness

A knowledge base that is not kept current is one of the most common causes of quality regression in production RAG integrations. Documents that are outdated produce confident but incorrect answers — more damaging than no answer at all. Establish a staleness monitoring process before go-live:
Each content area in the knowledge base has a named owner and a defined update frequency (daily, weekly, monthly, or event-triggered)
An automated staleness check runs nightly: documents that have not been reviewed within twice their update frequency are flagged for review
The operations team reviews the staleness report weekly and escalates flagged documents to the content owner
Retrieval Quality Monitoring

Retrieval quality is not directly visible in standard metrics (latency, error rate, cost). A RAG integration can have perfect uptime and low cost while producing poor answers because the retrieval is returning irrelevant chunks. Monitor retrieval quality explicitly:
Retrieval precision (P@K):

For a sample of production queries (sampled daily), have a reviewer assess whether the top-K retrieved chunks are relevant to the query. Track P@5 weekly. Alert if it falls below the threshold established during the pilot.
Context utilisation:

Analyse Claude's responses to determine whether the retrieved context is being used (cited, paraphrased) or ignored. High context-ignore rates suggest the retrieval is returning irrelevant chunks or the context window format is not optimal.
Out-of-scope query rate:

Track queries that are redirected by the scope enforcement layer. An increasing out-of-scope rate may indicate that users are trying to use the system for queries it cannot answer — the knowledge base may need to expand or the scope boundaries may need to be communicated more clearly.
Summary

The enterprise RAG reference architecture provides a blueprint for building a production-quality Claude integration that grounds responses in organisational knowledge. The core query path — API gateway, query processor, retriever, Claude API — must be implemented with attention to latency, access control, and scope enforcement. The ingestion pipeline must be designed for operational sustainability: automated ingestion, staleness detection, and re-indexing processes that keep the knowledge base current without constant manual intervention.

The quality of a RAG integration depends more on knowledge base design and retrieval quality than on the model choice or vector store technology. Investing in a sound chunking strategy, a comprehensive metadata schema, and systematic retrieval quality monitoring produces compounding returns — better retrieval quality means better answers, which means more user trust and adoption.
